title: Who won the 2025-26 NHL Calder Memorial Trophy (Rookie of the Year)?
rules: If Ivan Demidov wins the NHL Calder Memorial Trophy in the 2025-26 NHL season, then the market resolves to Yes.
tavily_brief: [redacted] **Matthew Schaefer's Dominance:** Schaefer, the first overall pick in the 2025 NHL Draft, has been the prohibitive favorite throughout the season. By late February, he held odds of -1000 (BetMGM), reflecting overwhelming confidence. The 18-year-old defenseman recorded 23 goals and 36 assists for 59 points across 82 games, rewriting NHL records for 18-year-old defensemen. He led all rookies in ice time (2,023:59), averaging over 24 minutes per game—more than 500 minutes ahead of the next-closest rookie. Schaefer became the first defenseman in over 90 years to lead rookies in goals and the youngest player in NHL history to score an overtime goal. His elite two-way play and power-play contributions made him the clear frontrunner. **Demidov's Strong Case:** Demidov entered 2025-26 as the pre-season favorite after posting 49 points in 65 KHL games and impressing in limited NHL action. The 19-year-old winger finished with 19 goals and 43 assists (62 points) in 82 games, leading all rookies in points. He was named NHL Rookie of the Month for December 2025 (4 goals, 10 assists in 15 games). Demidov's elite passing (1.78 assists per 60 at 5-on-5, sixth among all NHLers with 700+ minutes) and processing speed drew comparisons to top-tier playmakers. However, his odds drifted from +175 pre-season to +850 by late February as Schaefer's dominance became apparent. **Sennecke's Emergence:** The 19-year-old Ducks forward matched Schaefer with 23 goals and added 37 assists (60 points) in 82 games, ranking second among rookies. His even-strength production (21 goals, 46 points) led all rookies. Sennecke's physical 6-foot-3 frame combined with elite skill made him a unique talent, though his +1000 odds reflected his distant third position. **Key Context:** Schaefer's candidacy was strengthened by the Islanders' playoff success and the rarity of consecutive Calder winners at defenseman (last occurred in 1964). Demidov's Montreal team finished sixth overall in the NHL, partly due to his impact. Voting by the Professional Hockey Writers Association typically favors defensemen when they produce at elite levels—three of the last six Calder winners played defense. **Most Important Facts:** (1) Matthew Schaefer held overwhelming betting favorites (-1000 odds) and set multiple 18-year-old defenseman records with 59 points and 2,000+ ice time minutes. (2) Ivan Demidov led all rookies in points (62) with elite playmaking metrics but saw his odds drift significantly as Schaefer's dominance became clear. (3) The three finalists were announced May 5, 2026, with Schaefer's record-setting production and two-way excellence positioning him as the strong favorite entering the voting period.

args.reasoning:
Strong evidence converges on Matthew Schaefer winning. Key facts:

1. **May 5, 2026 NHL announcement**: Demidov, Schaefer, and Sennecke are the three Calder finalists — confirmed by multiple NHL.com and Sportsnet sources. This eliminates 27 of the 30 listed outcomes.

2. **Schaefer's historic season**: Named NHL Rookie of the Month for October (8 pts in 11 games, leading rookies in TOI and shots), continued strong production through November (5G, 6A, 11 pts, +7). Yahoo Sports (March 24, 2026) called it a "historic year" and notes he could become youngest Calder winner in NHL history as the 2024 #1 overall pick playing huge minutes as an 18-year-old defenseman.

3. **Market consensus**: Polymarket (per Lines.com April 1, 2026) had Schaefer at 98.3% implied probability with $286K volume and "no meaningful resistance on the NO side." Kalshi has him at 0.985.

4. **Demidov**: Solid rookie year for Montreal but clearly the #2 finalist with Kalshi at 0.03. Sennecke at 0.08 on Kalshi seems slightly elevated but reasonable as the dark horse.

The Calder is decided by PHWA voting completed at season's end — votes are essentially already cast by the time finalists are announced. Schaefer being a young #1 overall pick defenseman playing 25+ minutes a night for a competitive team checks every Calder voter box, and the market signal is overwhelming.

I'll set Schaefer at 0.95 (slightly below Kalshi's 0.985 to account for non-zero upset risk that voters surprise toward Demidov/Sennecke — historically Calder finalists not in #1 betting position have won occasionally), Demidov at 0.03, Sennecke at 0.015, and minimal mass on the rest as the finalist list eliminates them.

args.reasoning:
Evidence: On May 5, 2026, the NHL announced the three Calder Memorial Trophy finalists for 2025-26: Ivan Demidov (Canadiens), Matthew Schaefer (Islanders), and Beckett Sennecke (Ducks), per NHL.com’s official release (May 5, 2026). Sporting News’ finalists roundup the same day independently listed the same trio. The Calder winner is chosen from these PHWA-voted finalists; thus, the winner will be one of these three. No credible report of a winner announcement surfaced in my searches, indicating the decision had not been publicly released at the time of review. 

Implications: The finalist slate is decisive signal: all other named rookies are effectively out of contention once finalists are set. Within the finalist group, absent a published winner, we look to qualitative cues. A defenseman making the final three typically reflects an exceptional two-way or minutes-eating impact that resonates with PHWA voters; being the sole defenseman finalist therefore tilts toward Matthew Schaefer. Multiple mainstream outlets carried the same finalist list without any “runaway” narrative for a forward, which slightly weakens the case for a clear forward sweep by Demidov or Sennecke. Also, voting is based on regular-season performance and is locked before playoffs, so no late-breaking postseason events can swing the result after ballots are cast—there were no reports of controversies or disqualifying events affecting any finalist.

Upset check: No strong counter-signal (injury, scandal, endorsement flip) post-ballot emerged to justify shifting away from the finalist trio or to elevate one forward dramatically. Given only the finalists are viable, I concentrate probability on them, modestly leaning to Schaefer due to positional distinctiveness among finalists and typical PHWA appreciation for standout rookie defensemen, while leaving meaningful chances for Sennecke and Demidov.

Yahoo Sports (March 24, 2026) called it a "historic year" and notes he could become youngest Calder winner in NHL history as the 2024 #1 overall pick playing huge minutes as an 18-year-old defenseman.

3. **Market consensus**: Polymarket (per Lines.com April 1, 2026) had Schaefer at 98.3% implied probability with $286K volume and "no meaningful resistance on the NO side." Kalshi has him at 0.985.

4. **Demidov**: Solid rookie year for Montreal but clearly the #2 finalist with Kalshi at 0.03. Sennecke at 0.08 on Kalshi seems slightly elevated but reasonable as the dark horse.

The Calder is decided by PHWA voting completed at season's end — votes are essentially already cast by the time finalists are announced. Schaefer being a young #1 overall pick defenseman playing 25+ minutes a night for a competitive team checks every Calder voter box, and the market signal is overwhelming.

I'll set Schaefer at 0.95 (slightly below Kalshi's 0.985 to account for non-zero upset risk that voters surprise toward Demidov/Sennecke — historically Calder finalists not in #1 betting position have won occasionally), Demidov at 0.03, Sennecke at 0.015, and minimal mass on the rest as the finalist list eliminates them.

Evidence: On May 5, 2026, the NHL announced the three Calder Memorial Trophy finalists for 2025-26: Ivan Demidov (Canadiens), Matthew Schaefer (Islanders), and Beckett Sennecke (Ducks), per NHL.com’s official release (May 5, 2026). Sporting News’ finalists roundup the same day independently listed the same trio. The Calder winner is chosen from these PHWA-voted finalists; thus, the winner will be one of these three. No credible report of a winner announcement surfaced in my searches, indicating the decision had not been publicly released at the time of review. 

Implications: The finalist slate is decisive signal: all other named rookies are effectively out of contention once finalists are set. Within the finalist group, absent a published winner, we look to qualitative cues. A defenseman making the final three typically reflects an exceptional two-way or minutes-eating impact that resonates with PHWA voters; being the sole defenseman finalist therefore tilts toward Matthew Schaefer. Multiple mainstream outlets carried the same finalist list without any “runaway” narrative for a forward, which slightly weakens the case for a clear forward sweep by Demidov or Sennecke. Also, voting is based on regular-season performance and is locked before playoffs, so no late-breaking postseason events can swing the result after ballots are cast—there were no reports of controversies or disqualifying events affecting any finalist.

Upset check: No strong counter-signal (injury, scandal, endorsement flip) post-ballot emerged to justify shifting away from the finalist trio or to elevate one forward dramatically. Given only the finalists are viable, I concentrate probability on them, modestly leaning to Schaefer due to positional distinctiveness among finalists and typical PHWA appreciation for standout rookie defensemen, while leaving meaningful chances for Sennecke and Demidov.
